bibliographic.citation: Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady: very necessary for all relygyous persones ([London]: Richard Fawkes, 4 November 1530), STC 17542; ESTC S111821.
bibliographic.title: Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady

entities.description: The early printed edition completed on 4 November 1530 at the request of Abbess Agnes Jordan and general confessor John Fewterer. It is modeled under the schema's nearest edition type and should not be mistaken for a modern scholarly edition. Blunt's principal base copy was Bishop Cosin's Library, Durham, H.II.24; the printer's exemplar has not been established here.
entities.label: Richard Fawkes, Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady (1530)
notes: Blunt reports that Durham H.II.24 had a complete text but lacked the engraved title leaf and four pages of errata.
No claim is made that the surviving Tailour codex was Fawkes's printer's exemplar.

rights.basis: Published in 1530, centuries before modern U.S. copyright terms; modern photographs and provider terms are separate.
rights.component: 1530 printed text, typography, and historical edition content

next_steps: Ask each repository for any complete existing photography, microfilm, or unindexed digital surrogate before commissioning new work.

Request each entire present physical object, including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, adjacent material, parchment tags, and edge views if offered—not only the Myroure text or the CME portion.
Obtain exact current foliation and map Blunt's legacy manuscript pages 1–2 plus the CME text boundary within the complete deliveries.
Confirm private retention, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, print-on-demand publication, and commercial use separately in writing.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of both present parts of the historical Tailour codex—University of Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941—be obtained, retained locally, and mapped leaf by leaf?

next_steps: Request current foliation and complete image inventories from both repositories.
Crosswalk Blunt's 270-page legacy Aberdeen description and pages 1–2 sample to current folios.
Record first and final work leaves, any non-Myroure matter, the original split, and current binding boundaries without inferring them from printed pagination.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact current folio ranges and codicological join between the Myroure portions in Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941?

next_steps: Review Ann Hutchison's textual scholarship and the evidence for Thomas Fishbourne, Clement Maydeston, Simon Winter, and other proposed Syon authors.
Treat Blunt's Thomas Gascoigne proposal as a historical disputed attribution rather than established authorship.
Keep the conservative post-1415 to mid-fifteenth-century range until a source-based reassessment is complete.
openQuestions.question: Which anonymous Syon brother composed, translated, and commented on the Myroure, and how narrowly can its composition be dated?

next_steps: Do not infer that Fawkes used the surviving Tailour codex.
Audit complete surviving copies, including Durham H.II.24 and Cambridge A.B.10.41, against current catalogs and image services.
Ask Folger whether the copy 3 image set is cover-to-cover while remembering that copy 3 itself lacks all after R6.
Cache a complete provider-exposed physical copy only when its inventory and retention terms can be verified, and then map the CME edition portion within it.
openQuestions.question: What manuscript or textual exemplar did Fawkes use in 1530, and is there a verifiably complete public or licensable facsimile of a complete surviving copy?

next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source image package from U-M.
Confirm whether the manuscript-boundary selection was intentional and whether earlier CME phases preserve additional documentation.
Treat TEMPHEAD as evidence of 2016 validation and proofing rather than initial creation.
openQuestions.question: Why does CME00040 stop at the Aberdeen/Sunday half-volume boundary, and what initial keying, image, correction, and reuse history precedes the documented 2016 review?

next_steps: Collate Blunt's editorial conventions, the 1530 base print, and the Aberdeen pages 1–2 sample before interpreting italics uniformly.
Use complete-object facsimiles and exact work-portion mappings when they become available.
Distinguish Blunt's intervention from U-M XML rendition and repository PDF styling.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript or early-print features underlie every italicized expansion and other editorial intervention represented in CME00040?

reviewNotes: The two present manuscript objects are parts of one historical codex, but they are neither the same current physical object nor interchangeable facsimiles.
Complete-file coverage for the cached Blunt volume is distinguished from the discontinuous CME00040 work portion within that file.

A future manuscript cache should contain the complete provider-exposed physical object and use work_portion locators for the relevant leaves; it should not be reduced to the text-bearing leaves alone.

lineage.summary: CME00040 is not a direct manuscript transcription and does not contain the complete Myroure. The repository XML represents a U-M electronic selection from John Henry Blunt's 1873 EETS edition: the edition title page, Blunt's separate transcript of the first two pages of the Aberdeen manuscript, and printed pp. 1–174 through the Sunday service. Blunt's main edited text was based chiefly on Richard Fawkes's 1530 print, specifically the copy then and now cataloged at Bishop Cosin's Library, Durham, H.II.24; his Aberdeen transcript and consultation are recorded separately. The one historical Robert Tailour codex now survives as two present repository objects, University of Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941. No verifiable public cover-to-cover facsimile of either present manuscript object was found. This record therefore caches the complete public Blunt scan and exact catalog metadata, points to the work portions within broader sources, and records whole-volume reproduction requests without treating catalog records or selected images as manuscript facsimiles.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Syon brother; Thomas Fishbourne has been proposed
catalogSummary.date: Composed after Syon's 1415 foundation, probably in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1530; source edition 1873
catalogSummary.title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]

catalogingSubject.label: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus electronic selection from John Henry Blunt's 1873 EETS edition: the edition title page, Blunt's p. lx transcript of Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2, and printed pp. 1–174 through the Sunday service. It is not the complete medieval Myroure, not the complete 1530 print or 1873 edition, and not a direct transcription of either surviving manuscript part. Monday through Saturday, feasts, Part III, notes, and index are outside this unit.

contentParts.label: Blunt edition title page
contentParts.summary: The bibliographic title page of the 1873 EETS source edition, encoded as editorial paratext rather than medieval work text.

contentParts.label: Blunt's Aberdeen manuscript sample
notes: This sample should not be used to imply that the rest of CME00040 was transcribed from Aberdeen MS 134.
contentParts.summary: An editorially supplied transcript headed as pages 1 and 2 of the Aberdeen manuscript; it precedes the main printed text and is the only direct manuscript transcript established within CME00040.

contentParts.label: Two authorial prologues

locators.value: 1–9; the chapter table begins later on shared p. 9

contentParts.summary: Two opening prologues frame the purpose and audience of the Myroure before Part I.

contentParts.label: Table of the twenty-four Part I chapters
contentParts.summary: A chapter list ending with the heading that begins Part I; it shares boundary pages with the second prologue and first chapter.

contentParts.label: Part I: On divine service

locators.value: 10–65; heading begins on shared p. 10 and Part II begins on shared p. 65

contentParts.summary: Twenty-four numbered chapters explain the purpose, value, and disciplined performance of divine service for the Syon sisters.

contentParts.label: Part II: Instruction for reading

locators.value: 65–71; section begins on shared p. 65 and Sunday heading begins on shared p. 71

contentParts.summary: A prose instruction on how the sisters should read and understand their service before the weekday offices begin.

contentParts.label: Sunday service

locators.value: 71–174; heading begins on shared p. 71 and main text begins p. 72
145–248; Sunday heading begins on PDF p. 145 and main text begins p. 146

notes: Printed p. 174 is a textual boundary shared with the beginning of Monday in the complete edition; CME00040 stops after Sunday's closing statement.
contentParts.summary: The Sunday portion of the Bridgettine Office of the Virgin, translated and explained in sixty-four XML subdivisions, ending with Tailour's colophon that Sunday is sufficient for this half of the Myroure.

contentParts.label: Editorial note to the reader
contentParts.summary: A brief final editorial note following the Sunday-service close on p. 174; it is paratext, not another medieval work section.

assertion.notes: The terminus post quem is Syon's 1415 foundation. Modern discussion generally places the work in the first half of the fifteenth century, but exact authorship and date remain open.

notes: This is a composition range for the work, not a production date for either surviving manuscript part.

scope.description: Underlying Middle English Myroure represented by the selection

notes: This date belongs to the early print underlying Blunt's principal text, not to medieval composition.

scope.description: First known printed edition of the complete Myroure
Editorial presentation from which the electronic selection is encoded

extent.description: The XML has 8 top-level DIV1 containers—1 in FRONT and 7 in BODY—and 90 DIV2 containers: 2 prologues, 24 numbered Part I chapters, and 64 Sunday-service subdivisions. It also has 176 distinct PB references, 127 page/folio/leaf milestones, 111 P elements, 845 NOTE elements, and no LG or L verse hierarchy. The encoded print coverage is the edition title page, p. lx, and pp. 1–174; these are discontinuous in the complete scan. Counts describe XML/editorial structure rather than a canonical chapter count for the complete medieval work.

formStatements.description: The selected medieval work is prose; embedded Latin liturgical phrases and incipits do not make the cataloged unit a mixed prose-and-verse work.

genres.label: Vernacular religious translation
notes: The liturgical section translates and explains the Bridgettine office; it is not merely a word-for-word vernacular version.

scope.description: Medieval treatise, instructions, translation, and commentary
Liturgical incipits, rubrics, quotations, and source phrases embedded in the Middle English prose
Blunt's title, headings, notes, introductory material, and supplied labels

notes: The preferred title's bracketed qualifier is supplied for this electronic selection and is not asserted as a medieval title form.
Composition, manuscript production, first printing, scholarly edition, U-M encoding, and repository representation are separate dates and layers.
Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941 are two present physical objects from one historical codex; neither current shelfmark should be used as a synonym for the other.
The direct source for most CME00040 text is Blunt's edition of the 1530 print. Only the p. lx sample directly transcribes Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2.
Exact facsimile availability, whole-object request scope, cache records, image rights, and publication or commercial-use constraints are maintained in the paired lineage manifest.

assertion.notes: The work is securely associated with the Syon community, but a precise room or continuous site of composition is not documented.

notes: This records the work's Bridgettine institutional setting, not the later locations of the split manuscript parts or the 1873 publication place.

assertion.notes: The work was produced for the Bridgettine community at Syon, but no author is securely named.

assertion.notes: The Myroure combines translation with explanation; the entire selected unit should not be reduced to a bare translation.

scope.description: The 1873 source-edition presentation selected by U-M
Instruction for the Bridgettine sisters' communal religious observance

summaries.value: The Myroure of Oure Ladye is an anonymous Middle English devotional and liturgical work made for the Bridgettine sisters of Syon Abbey. It explains why and how the divine office should be performed, instructs readers in attentive reading, and translates and comments on the community's Office of the Virgin, the Cantus sororum. CME00040 presents only the opening authorial material, Part I, reading instructions, and the Sunday office, framed by Blunt's title page and a short Aberdeen manuscript sample.

The electronic unit contains the 1873 title page; Blunt's p. lx transcript of legacy Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2; two authorial prologues; a chapter table; Part I in twenty-four chapters on divine service; Part II reading instructions; the Sunday service through the colophon saying that Sunday is sufficient for this half; and a final editorial note to the reader. It omits the remaining weekday offices, feasts, Part III masses, and Blunt's later apparatus.

Although the electronic selection ends at the same Sunday boundary as the present Aberdeen half of the split manuscript, its main text is encoded from Blunt's 1873 edition, whose base is the 1530 Fawkes print. Only the p. lx sample directly transcribes Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2.

assertion.notes: The bracketed qualifier distinguishes the electronic selection from the complete medieval work and complete Blunt edition.

titles.value: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]

assertion.notes: The title belongs bibliographically to Blunt's complete edition; CME00040 includes its title page but only a selection of its text.

titles.value: Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady: very necessary for all relygyous persones

work.author: Anonymous Syon brother; Thomas Fishbourne has been proposed
work.dateDisplay: Composed after Syon's 1415 foundation, probably in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1530; source edition 1873

CME00040 presents only the opening authorial material, Part I, reading instructions, and the Sunday office, framed by Blunt's title page and a short Aberdeen manuscript sample.
